1: A theme: "In you, O LORD, I take refuge; let me never be put to shame." The opening of Psalm 71 nearly quotes Psalms 31:1 verbatim — the same refuge-claim and same shame-negation. The psalmist appears to be an elderly David (Psalms 71:9, 18) revisiting the lament tradition of his youth. Romans 10:11 (citing Isaiah 28:16): "anyone who trusts in him will never be put to shame" — the "never be put to shame" of the lament tradition becomes the covenant promise of the gospel, grounded in trust in Christ. The refuge that the psalmist takes in YHWH is the refuge available to all in Christ, who is described as the "sure and steadfast anchor for the soul" (Hebrews 6:19).
2: A theme: "In your righteousness deliver me and rescue me; incline your ear to me and save me." The grounding of rescue in divine ṣĕdāqāh (righteousness) — not human merit but divine covenant faithfulness — is the OT foundation for Paul's concept of the "righteousness of God" in Romans. Romans 3:21-22: "the righteousness of God has been made known... through faith in Jesus Christ to all who believe." The deliverance and rescue that the psalmist appeals to through divine righteousness is what Christ accomplishes by being "made our righteousness" (1 Corinthians 1:30). The ear that inclines toward the cry of the righteous is the ear of the Father who "heard" Christ "because of his reverence" (Hebrews 5:7).
3: A theme: "Be for me a sheltering rock, a place I can always come to; you have commanded my salvation, for you are my rock and my fortress." The rock-fortress that is "always available" — the permanent, commanded salvation — is the security that Christ provides for those in him. Matthew 16:18: "on this rock I will build my church, and the gates of death will not overcome it." 1 Corinthians 10:4 identifies the rock with Christ directly. The "commanded salvation" (ṣiwwîtā lehôšîa'nî) — salvation that comes by divine decree, not human effort — is the salvation given in Christ that the believer receives rather than achieves.
4: A theme: "Rescue me, O my God, from the hand of the wicked, from the grip of the unjust and cruel." The threefold enemy — the wicked, the unjust, the cruel — is the social and political reality of the righteous sufferer. The passion narrative embodies all three: the wicked (those who plotted; Mark 14:1), the unjust (Pilate, who knew Jesus was innocent; Luke 23:22), and the cruel (the mockers and soldiers of the crucifixion; Matthew 27:27-31). Hebrews 5:7 (Christ "offered up prayers... to him who was able to save him from death") is the NT echo of this rescue-plea from the hand of the unjust.
5: A theme: "For you, O Lord GOD, are my hope, my trust from my earliest years." Trust rooted in earliest life — the faith formed in youth and carried through old age — is the model of perseverance the NT commends. 2 Timothy 1:5 (Timothy's faith, "which lived first in your grandmother Lois and in your mother Eunice") and Hebrews 11 (the catalogue of those who trusted "from their earliest years" through to death) develop this pattern. Christ himself trusted the Father "from his earliest years" (Luke 2:49: "I had to be in my Father's house") through to the cross.
6: A shadow: "I have depended on you from before I was born; you drew me safely out of my mother's womb. My praise is forever of you." The prenatal divine protection and birth as acts of divine faithfulness — the pattern of Psalms 22:9-10 and Isaiah 49:1 (the Servant: "before I was born the LORD called me") — is the shadow behind which Christ's conception and birth stand. Luke 1:35 (the Holy Spirit overshadowing Mary; the holy one conceived) and the Emmanuel-child of Isaiah 7:14 are the specific nativity fulfillments. The praise that begins before birth and extends forever anticipates the eternal praise of the Lamb (Revelation 5:12-13).
7: A shadow: "I have been like an astonishing sign (môfēt) to many, but you are my strong shelter." The psalmist as a "sign/portent" to observers — a person whose suffering and trust in God makes them a visible object of wonder — is the pattern that Hebrews 2:13 applies to Christ. Hebrews 2:13 cites Isaiah 8:18 ("here am I, and the children God has given me" — the Servant and his community as signs from YHWH), placing it in Christ's mouth. Christ is the ultimate môfēt: the one whose life, death, and resurrection made him "a sign that will be spoken against" (Luke 2:34) and the astonishing portent of divine power over death (Acts 2:22: "accredited by God to you by miracles, wonders and signs").
8: A theme: "My mouth is full of your praise and your glory all the day long." The mouth perpetually filled with praise — the posture of sustained doxology — is the disposition Christ cultivates in his disciples (John 15:11: "that your joy may be complete") and that the redeemed community practices (Hebrews 13:15: "continually offer to God a sacrifice of praise — the fruit of lips that openly profess his name"). The "all the day long" of continuous praise anticipates the unceasing worship of the new creation: "day and night they never stop saying: 'Holy, holy, holy is the Lord God Almighty'" (Revelation 4:8).
9: A theme: "Do not reject me when old age comes; do not abandon me when my strength gives out." The fear of divine abandonment in weakness and aging — the request that divine faithfulness extend through the final stages of life — is the prayer that Christ's promise answers: "I will never leave you nor forsake you" (Hebrews 13:5, citing Deuteronomy 31:6, 8). The "strength giving out" (kōhî kalōt) is the human physical diminishment that makes divine sustaining grace most necessary. 2 Corinthians 12:9: "my grace is sufficient for you, for my power is made perfect in weakness." The God who does not abandon in old age is the Christ who says "I am with you always, to the very end of the age" (Matthew 28:20).
10: A theme: "For my enemies speak about me; those who watch for my life conspire together." The conspiracy of enemies who discuss and plot against the righteous sufferer — their words as weapons before their acts — is the pattern of Jesus's Passion. Mark 14:1 ("the chief priests and the teachers of the law were looking for a way to arrest Jesus secretly and kill him") and John 11:53 ("from that day on they plotted to take his life") are the literal fulfillments. The "watching for my life" (šōmĕrê nafšî — those who guard/watch for a life to take it) is the hostile surveillance that surrounded Jesus's final week.
11: A direct anticipation: "They say, 'God has abandoned him; run him down and take him, for there is no one to rescue him.'" This verse is a striking direct anticipation of the mockery at the cross. Matthew 27:43: "He trusts in God. Let God rescue him now if he wants him, for he said, 'I am the Son of God.'" (Matthew's wording echoes Psalms 22:8 as well, but the theme of "God has abandoned him" and "no one to rescue" is Psalms 71:11's exact content.) Luke 23:35 describes bystanders who "sneered" as Jesus hung on the cross. The taunters of Psalms 71:11 are the taunters of Golgotha; the righteous sufferer who is mocked as God-forsaken is Christ, whom God does not ultimately abandon (the resurrection vindicates the trust).
12: A theme: "O God, do not be far from me; O my God, come quickly to my aid." The double petition — nearness and urgency — is the cry of the one who senses the divine withdrawal that accompanies deep suffering. The same cry appears in Psalms 22:11, 19 and is the template for the Gethsemane prayer (Matthew 26:36-46). Hebrews 4:16: "let us then approach God's throne of grace with confidence, so that we may receive mercy and find grace to help us in our time of need." Christ is both the one who prayed this cry (in his earthly agony) and the one who ensures it is answered (as the intercessor and high priest at God's right hand).
13: A theme: "Let those who attack my life be put to shame and consumed; let those who seek my harm be wrapped in reproach and dishonor." The imprecation against persecutors — the prayer for divine reversal of their plans — is covenant realism: the moral order requires that violence against the righteous be answered. The NT does not repudiate this realism but transforms its timing and agent: Romans 12:19 ("do not take revenge... 'It is mine to avenge; I will repay'"); and the eschatological judgment of Revelation 19-20 where those who made war against Christ and his people receive "reproach and dishonor." The putting-to-shame of the persecutors is guaranteed by the resurrection, which already vindicates the suffering righteous.
14: A theme: "But I will hope continually and will praise you more and more." The resolve of unceasing hope — hope that increases rather than diminishes under pressure — is the posture that Romans 5:3-5 describes as the fruit of suffering: "suffering produces perseverance; perseverance, character; character, hope. And hope does not put us to shame." Hebrews 12:1-3 grounds this increasing hope in "fixing our eyes on Jesus, the pioneer and perfecter of faith, who for the joy set before him endured the cross." The psalmist's "more and more" praise is what Christ's perseverance through suffering models and his Spirit enables.
15: A theme: "My mouth will proclaim your righteousness and your saving acts all the day long, for I cannot count their number." The uncountable divine saving acts — the surplus of righteousness and deliverance that exceeds calculation — is the theme that Hebrews 11 catalogs across the OT and that Paul alludes to in Romans 8:28-30. But the single saving act that encompasses all others — "reconciling the world to himself" (2 Corinthians 5:19) — is Christ's death and resurrection. The "righteousness" proclaimed in v15 is the ṣĕdāqôt (righteousnesses/righteous acts) of YHWH, fulfilled and concentrated in the one act that Romans calls "the righteousness of God" (Romans 1:17; Psalms 3:21-22).
16: A theme: "I will come in the strength of the Lord GOD; I will declare your righteousness — yours and no one else's." The exclusivity claim — "yours and no one else's" (ki-attah levaddeka, for you alone) — is the monotheistic insistence that righteousness belongs to YHWH alone, not to human achievement. Romans 3:10-12 establishes that no human is righteous; Romans 3:21-26 then declares that YHWH's own righteousness is given through Christ. The "declaring your righteousness alone" of Psalms 71:16 is fulfilled in the apostolic proclamation that Christ's righteousness, not human merit, is the basis of salvation (Philippians 3:9: "not having a righteousness of my own... but that which is through faith in Christ").
17: A revelation of God: "O God, you have taught me from my youth, and to this day I am still declaring your wonderful deeds." The lifelong divine instruction — God as teacher from youth through old age — is the relationship that Christ embodies perfectly and mediates for others. John 13:13: "you call me 'Teacher' and 'Lord,' and rightly so, for that is what I am." Matthew 23:10: "you have one Teacher, the Messiah." The "wonderful deeds" (nifle-otekha) that the psalmist declares are ultimately the miracles and wonders of Christ (Acts 2:22), which the disciples proclaimed throughout their lives.
18: A theme: "And even now in old age, with gray hair, O God, do not forsake me until I have proclaimed your strength to this generation and your power to all who are to come." The missional urgency of old age — to declare God's strength to the next generation before dying — is the pattern of the apostolic testimony. 2 Peter 1:13-15: "I think it is right to refresh your memory as long as I live in the tent of this body, because I know that I will soon put it aside... I will make every effort to see that after my departure you will always be able to remember these things." The transmission of divine strength-stories to future generations is the catechetical task that the apostolic church inherited and that the written NT preserves.
19: A revelation of God: "Your righteousness, O God, reaches the high heavens; you who have done such great things, O God — who is like you?" The incomparability of YHWH — the "who is like you?" (mî kāmôkā) that is the OT's monotheistic challenge to all rivals — is applied in the NT to Christ. Revelation 1:8 (Christ: "I am the Alpha and the Omega, says the Lord God, who is, and who was, and who is to come, the Almighty") and Colossians 1:16-17 ("all things were created through him and for him... in him all things hold together") deploy the incomparability claims to include the Son. The righteousness that "reaches the high heavens" is the righteousness of Christ, which covers "all who believe" (Romans 3:22).
20: A direct anticipation: "You who have made me experience many bitter troubles will revive me again; from the depths of the earth you will bring me up once more." The resurrection pattern stated explicitly: YHWH who permitted bitter troubles will revive (tĕhayy-ênî, give life again) and bring up (ta'ălēnî, raise) from the depths of the earth. Acts 2:24: "God raised him from the dead, freeing him from the agony of death, because it was impossible for death to keep its hold on him." This verse is one of the clearest OT statements of the resurrection hope applied to an individual — and the NT reads the same pattern in Christ's death and resurrection. The "depths of the earth" (tĕhōmôt hā'āreṣ) parallels Ephesians 4:9's reference to Christ's descent to "the lower, earthly regions."
21: A theme: "You will increase my honor and surround me with comfort." The restoration of honor after humiliation — the divine vindication that follows the suffering — is the pattern of the Christological journey: "for the joy set before him, he endured the cross, scorning its shame, and sat down at the right hand of the throne of God" (Hebrews 12:2). Philippians 2:8-9: "he humbled himself by becoming obedient to death — even death on a cross! Therefore God exalted him to the highest place." The increase of honor in Psalms 71:21 is the exaltation that the resurrection and ascension enact for Christ — the Father's vindication of the humiliated Son.
22: A revelation of God: "I will praise you with the lyre for your faithfulness, O my God; I will sing to you with the harp, O Holy One of Israel." "The Holy One of Israel" (qĕdôš yiśrā'ēl) is Isaiah's distinctive title for YHWH — used 25 times in Isaiah, emphasizing the transcendent holiness of the covenant God. Mark 1:24 and Luke 4:34 report a demon addressing Jesus as "the Holy One of God" (ho hagios tou theou) — recognizing in him the same divine holiness Isaiah attributed to YHWH. John 6:69: Peter confesses "we have come to believe and to know that you are the Holy One of God." The praise due the Holy One of Israel in Psalms 71:22 is due to Christ.
23: A theme: "My lips will shout for joy when I sing praises to you; and my soul, which you have redeemed, will also rejoice." The redeemed soul's joy — the integration of lips and soul in praise — is the NT ideal of authentic worship. John 4:24 ("God is spirit, and his worshipers must worship in the Spirit and in truth"); Ephesians 5:19 ("sing and make music from your heart to the Lord"). "My soul, which you have redeemed" — nafšî 'ăšer pādîtāh — the personal claim of redemption that grounds joy. 1 Peter 1:18-19: "redeemed... with the precious blood of Christ." The soul redeemed by Christ's blood is the soul whose joy is anchored in the redemption already accomplished.
24: A theme: "And my tongue will speak of your righteous deeds all day long, for those who sought to harm me have been put to shame and humiliated." The closing verse gathers the psalm's two threads: continuous proclamation of divine righteousness and the final shame of persecutors. The NT's eschatological form: Philippians 2:10-11 ("at the name of Jesus every knee should bow... and every tongue acknowledge that Jesus Christ is Lord") is the universal proclamation of the righteousness of God in Christ, before which even those who "sought to harm" the righteous sufferer will ultimately bow. The shame of the psalmist's enemies becomes the shame of all who opposed Christ at the final judgment.
